A dental crown is a tooth-shaped cap placed over a tooth to restore its shape, size, and strength.
Crowns are typically used after a root canal to protect the remaining tooth structure.
Common crown materials include porcelain, ceramic, zirconia, and metal alloys.
A dental bridge replaces one or more missing teeth by anchoring to adjacent natural teeth.
Dental implants are titanium posts placed in the jawbone to replace tooth roots.
Implants integrate with the bone over several months in a process called osseointegration.
A dental filling is used to repair a tooth damaged by decay back to its normal shape and function.
Composite fillings are tooth-colored and bond directly to the tooth structure.
Amalgam fillings are silver-colored and have been used safely for over a century.
Routine dental cleanings remove plaque and tartar that brushing and flossing miss.
Brushing twice daily with fluoride toothpaste helps prevent cavities and gum disease.
Flossing once a day removes food debris between teeth where a toothbrush cannot reach.
Dental x-rays help dentists see inside teeth and below the gum line.
Modern digital x-rays use up to ninety percent less radiation than traditional film x-rays.
Cavities form when bacteria in the mouth produce acids that erode tooth enamel.
Early-stage cavities can sometimes be reversed with fluoride treatments and improved oral hygiene.
Gum disease, also called periodontitis, can lead to tooth loss if left untreated.
Symptoms of gum disease include red, swollen, or bleeding gums.
Regular dental visits every six months allow early detection of oral health issues.
A dental veneer is a thin shell of porcelain bonded to the front of a tooth for cosmetic improvement.
Teeth whitening can be done in-office or with at-home kits prescribed by a dentist.
Wisdom teeth, the third molars, often need to be removed if they cause crowding or impaction.
Orthodontic treatment with braces or aligners corrects misaligned teeth and bites.
Clear aligners are removable and offer a more discreet option than traditional metal braces.
A night guard protects teeth from grinding during sleep, a condition called bruxism.
Mouth guards worn during sports help prevent dental injuries to the teeth and jaw.
Fluoride strengthens tooth enamel and helps prevent decay.
Children's dental care should begin around the time the first tooth appears.
Sealants are a protective coating applied to chewing surfaces of back teeth in children.
Sensitive teeth can be caused by worn enamel, exposed roots, or recent dental work.
Toothpaste for sensitive teeth contains compounds that block pain signals from the tooth surface.
Dry mouth can increase the risk of tooth decay because saliva helps neutralize acids.
Sugar-free gum stimulates saliva flow and can help reduce cavity risk between meals.
A toothache that lasts more than a day or two should be evaluated by a dentist promptly.
Severe tooth pain accompanied by fever or facial swelling may indicate an abscess.
A dental abscess is a pocket of pus caused by bacterial infection and requires immediate treatment.
Antibiotics may be prescribed alongside dental procedures to control infection.
Local anesthesia numbs the area being treated so dental procedures are comfortable.
Sedation dentistry options range from mild relaxation to full sleep for anxious patients.
Dental insurance typically covers preventive care fully and restorative work partially.
Tooth enamel is the hardest substance in the human body but cannot regenerate once damaged.
Dentin lies beneath the enamel and contains microscopic tubules that lead to the tooth's nerve.
The pulp at the center of a tooth contains blood vessels and nerves.
Root canal treatment removes infected pulp and seals the inside of the tooth.
Most root canal procedures save the natural tooth and last for many years.
Tooth extractions are performed when a tooth cannot be saved by other means.
After an extraction, a blood clot forms in the socket as the first step of healing.
Dry socket is a painful complication that occurs when the protective blood clot is dislodged.
Bone grafting may be needed before implant placement if the jawbone has receded.
A sinus lift is sometimes performed in the upper jaw to make room for implants.
Dentures are removable replacements for missing teeth and surrounding tissues.
Partial dentures fill in the spaces created by missing teeth without removing existing ones.
Full dentures replace all teeth in either the upper or lower jaw.
Implant-supported dentures snap onto dental implants for greater stability than conventional dentures.
Cleaning dentures daily with a soft brush and denture cleanser helps prevent staining and odor.
Bad breath, or halitosis, is often caused by bacteria on the tongue or in the throat.
Brushing the tongue and using mouthwash can reduce odor-causing bacteria.
Pregnancy can increase the risk of gum disease due to hormonal changes.
Diabetic patients face higher risks for gum disease and slower oral healing.
Smoking significantly increases the risk of gum disease, oral cancer, and tooth loss.
Routine oral cancer screenings during dental visits can catch warning signs early.
Sores in the mouth that don't heal within two weeks should be evaluated by a dentist.
TMJ disorders affect the joint that connects the jaw to the skull.
Symptoms of TMJ include jaw pain, clicking sounds, and difficulty chewing.
Treatments for TMJ range from jaw exercises to night guards to physical therapy.
Cracked tooth syndrome is pain caused by a fracture too small to see on x-rays.
A cracked tooth may need a crown or root canal depending on the severity.
Dental tourism, traveling abroad for dental care, requires careful research on provider credentials.
Pediatric dentists specialize in caring for the dental needs of children and adolescents.
Endodontists specialize in treating problems inside the tooth, primarily root canal therapy.
Periodontists focus on the gums and structures supporting the teeth.
Orthodontists specialize in correcting misaligned teeth and jaws.
Oral surgeons perform extractions, implant placement, and corrective jaw surgery.
Prosthodontists specialize in dental restorations like crowns, bridges, and dentures.
Cosmetic dentistry focuses on the appearance of teeth and includes whitening, veneers, and bonding.
Tooth bonding repairs chips, cracks, or gaps using a tooth-colored composite resin.
Inlays and onlays are partial crowns used when damage is too large for a filling but smaller than a full crown.
Modern dental impressions can be taken digitally using a small intraoral scanner.
Same-day crowns are possible with CAD/CAM technology that mills crowns in the office.
3D printing is increasingly used for dental models, surgical guides, and temporary restorations.
Tele-dentistry connects patients with dentists remotely for consultation and triage.
Children should learn to brush with parental supervision until about age seven.
Limit sugary drinks and snacks between meals to reduce cavity risk.
Drinking water after meals helps rinse away food particles and acids.
A balanced diet rich in calcium and vitamin D supports strong teeth and bones.
Avoid using teeth as tools to open packages or bottle caps to prevent fractures.
Replace your toothbrush every three to four months or sooner if bristles are frayed.
Electric toothbrushes can be more effective at removing plaque than manual brushing for some users.
Use floss or interdental brushes daily to clean between teeth.
Mouthwash with fluoride adds extra protection against cavities for high-risk patients.
After dental treatment, follow your dentist's instructions to ensure proper healing.
Avoid smoking, alcohol, and hard foods immediately following oral surgery.
Most dental procedures involve only mild discomfort that resolves within a day or two.
Severe pain, prolonged bleeding, or worsening swelling after a procedure should be reported to your dentist.
Schedule your child's first dental visit by the first birthday or within six months of the first tooth.
Establishing a dental home early in life sets the foundation for lifelong oral health.
Dental anxiety is common; honest communication with your dental team can help manage it.
Many dental offices offer noise-cancelling headphones, blankets, or other comforts to ease anxiety.
Preventive care is the most cost-effective way to maintain long-term oral health.
Combining good home care with regular professional cleanings keeps teeth and gums healthy for life.
